text.title: RAG Pipeline Design: Architecture and Component Selection
text.summary: Architecture tiers, component stack selection, vector database comparison, and chunking strategy matrix for production RAG pipelines.

rule.statement: Select the architecture tier based on query complexity: Naive RAG for simple repetitive Q&A, Advanced RAG for ambiguous user queries, Modular RAG for multiple data sources, Agentic RAG for multi-step reasoning and tool use.
rule.rationale: Starting with Naive RAG and upgrading incrementally prevents over-engineering. Each tier adds infrastructure cost: Advanced RAG adds a reranking call; Modular RAG adds orchestration; Agentic RAG adds agent loops with LLM calls per retrieval round.

list.item: Naive RAG: fixed-size chunks → vector search → generate; suits FAQ and policy Q&A
Advanced RAG: query rewriting + HyDE + hybrid search + reranking; suits ambiguous queries
Modular RAG: pluggable components, parallel retrieval from heterogeneous sources
Agentic RAG: iterative retrieval loops, reformulation, multi-source synthesis

rule.statement: Use Chroma for local development, Qdrant for production self-hosted, Pinecone for managed serverless. Choose pgvector only when a PostgreSQL database already exists and operational simplicity outweighs performance.
rule.rationale: Qdrant is written in Rust with built-in hybrid search and rich metadata filtering — the best self-hosted option. Pinecone auto-scales with no operational burden. pgvector is limited to 2000 dimensions and lacks native hybrid search.

list.item: Pinecone: managed enterprise, auto-scaling, multi-region, SOC 2, 40KB metadata/vector
Qdrant: self-hosted production, Rust performance, hybrid search, rich filtering
Weaviate: knowledge graphs, GraphQL API, hybrid search, modules ecosystem
Milvus: large-scale (>100M vectors), GPU acceleration, distributed
Chroma: local development, simple API, not for multi-process production
pgvector: existing PostgreSQL, familiar tooling, limited to 2000 dims

rule.statement: Use Voyage-3-large for quality-critical applications (outperforms OpenAI by 9-20% on retrieval benchmarks). Use text-embedding-3-small for cost-sensitive deployments. Use local BGE or E5 models when data privacy requires no external API calls.
rule.rationale: Embedding model choice directly determines retrieval recall ceiling. Switching models after index build requires full re-indexing — decide once and commit. Voyage-3-large is the current MTEB leader for retrieval tasks.

rule.statement: Use RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter at 400-512 tokens with 50-token overlap as the default. Apply semantic chunking for long-form articles where topic boundaries matter. Use hierarchical chunking for technical manuals that mix high-level context with specific details.
rule.rationale: NVIDIA benchmark: page-level chunking achieves 0.648 accuracy. Semantic chunking improves recall by 9% over fixed-size. RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter at 400-512 tokens achieves 85-90% recall — a reliable default before tuning.

list.item: Short, single-purpose (FAQs, tickets): no chunking / document-level
Technical manuals: hierarchical, parent 1000 tokens / child 200 tokens

text.reference: Production RAG with Qdrant hybrid search
